era_context: Egypt's Old Kingdom, Dynasty IV (c. 2613–2494 BCE). The reign of Khufu or his immediate successors; the age of the great pyramid complexes at Giza. Contemporaneously, the Akkad Empire was consolidating power in Mesopotamia under Sargon of Akkad (r. c. 2334–2279 BCE), though this is slightly later. In the Indus Valley, early urban centres were developing. This is the height of Egyptian monumental architecture and centralised pharaonic power.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Mesopotamia (Akkad/Sumeria) — limited specific events for this year
Indus Valley Civilisation — no written records survive; only archaeological evidence
Sub-Saharan Africa — virtually no contemporary documentation

events.title: Khufu's reign or successor's reign; continued pyramid construction at Giza
events.description: The Great Pyramid of Khufu (Cheops) was completed c. 2560 BCE, but the broader Giza complex continued to be expanded and refined during the reigns of Khufu's successors (Khafre, Menkaure) through the mid-26th century. By 2589 BCE, if we place this in Khufu's reign or shortly after, the Giza plateau was the centre of Egyptian monumental ambition. The construction involved tens of thousands of workers, sophisticated quarrying, transport, and architectural knowledge. This represents the apex of Old Kingdom pharaonic power and resource mobilisation.
key_figures: Khufu (Pharaoh, r. c. 2589–2566 BCE, traditional dating)
Khafre (Pharaoh, r. c. 2558–2532 BCE, if this year falls in his reign)

sources.name: Abydos King List (Temple of Seti I, 19th Dynasty); Manetho's Aegyptiaca (later compilation, 3rd century BCE)
Archaeological evidence from Giza pyramid complex (excavations by Petrie, Reisner, Lehner, and others)

events.certainty_note: Egyptian chronology for the Old Kingdom is debated. Traditional dating places Khufu c. 2589–2566 BCE, but some scholars propose dates 50–100 years lower. The year 2589 BCE is assigned by convention to Khufu's accession in the most widely used chronology (Dodson & Hilton, Manetho-based), but uncertainty is ±20–30 years.

disconfirming_evidence: Egyptian Old Kingdom chronology is one of the most contested areas in ancient history. The 'low chronology' (favoured by some scholars, e.g., Baines & Malek) places Khufu c. 2509–2483 BCE, roughly 80 years lower than the traditional 'high chronology' used here. Radiocarbon dating of organic material from the pyramids has produced inconsistent results, some supporting the low chronology. The Abydos King List and Manetho's king list, our primary sources for regnal lengths, are themselves problematic: Manetho's work survives only in quotations by later authors (Josephus, Eusebius), and the Abydos list omits some rulers and may conflate others. No contemporary written records from Khufu's reign survive; all dating is inferential.
historiographic_note: The historical record for this year is almost entirely dependent on Egyptian king lists compiled centuries or millennia after the fact, and on archaeological stratigraphy and artefact analysis. There are no contemporary written records from Egypt for this period—the earliest substantial administrative records date to Dynasty V (c. 2494–2345 BCE). The Giza pyramids themselves are the primary 'text,' but they do not bear dates. Bias: Egyptian sources are heavily weighted toward the Nile Valley and royal monuments; the lives of ordinary people, regional variation, and non-Egyptian civilisations are largely invisible. The 'Old Kingdom' label itself is a modern historiographic construct, not an ancient one.

graph_edges.note: Giza pyramid complex construction spans multiple reigns; completion of Great Pyramid c. 2560 BCE
Old Kingdom pyramid-building tradition continues until Dynasty V decline
